name: Ghost Frequencies
description: A distress signal crackles through a frequency only Kos knows — one used by the spacer families who raised him. Someone is alive. Someone the Empire already erased from the records. But following that signal means lighting up every Imperial sensor net in the sector.
hopePole: The Open Hand — reach for everyone, even when it costs you the shadows that keep you alive.
tollPole: The Ghost Protocol — stay invisible, save who you can carry, and leave the rest to silence.
setup: You're running dark through the Tertiary Hyperlane when the signal hits your receiver — a burst-code pattern your mother taught you before you could walk. Three short, two long, one short. Family in trouble. The encryption is old, sloppy. Anyone scanning within four parsecs has already heard it. Your navicomputer traces the origin: a derelict refueling platform in the Atravis Sector, right on the edge of an Imperial patrol corridor. The smart move is to keep flying. You've survived this long by being smart. But that code means children. It always means children.

rounds.prompt: You drop out of hyperspace at the platform's edge. Scanners show two things: a cluster of life signs in the lower decks — maybe fifteen people, mostly small — and an Imperial Gozanti patrol cruiser forty minutes out, running a standard sweep. Forty minutes to dock, find them, and jump. Or you could shadow the cruiser's blind spot and slip past without stopping.
rounds.narrativeContext: The platform is dark. No running lights, no beacon. Someone cut the power deliberately — hiding. Your ship's profile is clean, unregistered. The moment you dock, that changes.

choices.label: Dock immediately — forty minutes is enough if I don't hesitate
choices.narration: You bring the ship in hard, locking clamps before the dust settles. The clock starts. Forty minutes. Your hands are steady but your heart isn't — you know what forty minutes feels like when everything goes wrong.

choices.label: Broadcast a narrow-band response on the family frequency — if they can move, tell them to meet me at the upper airlock in ten
choices.narration: You key the old frequency. Three long, one short. Coming. Move to upper decks. You're giving them a chance without fully committing. Smart. Efficient. Cold, if you think about it too long.

choices.label: Shadow the cruiser's blind spot — I can come back after the patrol passes
choices.narration: You adjust course, sliding into the cruiser's sensor shadow like a second skin. Professional. Clean. The signal keeps pulsing against your hull. Three short, two long, one short. You can still hear it as you pull away.

rounds.prompt: Inside the platform, you find them: spacer families huddled in a cargo hold that smells like recycled air and fear. Mostly Duros, a few humans, some children too young to understand why they're hiding. An old navigator named Threel recognizes your face — says you look like your mother. Then he tells you the bad news: three of their people went to the reactor level to restore power for a distress beacon. They haven't come back. The reactor level is flooded with coolant gas.
rounds.narrativeContext: Threel's hands are shaking. The children are quiet in the way that means they've learned silence as survival. Your breath mask is rated for coolant gas. Theirs aren't.

choices.label: I go down to the reactor level — I have the mask and the training, and those three people are counting on someone coming
choices.narration: You strap on the mask, check the seal twice, and drop into the green haze. The coolant burns your exposed skin where the suit gaps. You can hear coughing below. Someone is still alive down there.

choices.label: Start moving everyone who can walk to my ship now — I'll come back for the reactor team if there's time
choices.narration: You start herding people toward the airlock. Small hands grip your jumpsuit as you walk. Behind you, the reactor level hums and leaks. You're doing the math: fifteen people times the distance to your cargo bay, divided by the minutes you have left.

choices.label: Seal the reactor level — the coolant will contain itself if we lock the blast doors, and we need to move now
choices.narration: You find the blast door controls. Three keystrokes and the reactor level goes quiet. Sealed. Contained. Efficient. Threel watches you do it and says nothing, but his eyes say everything. You tell yourself they were probably already gone. You're probably right.

rounds.prompt: You're loading the last of the refugees onto your ship when the sensor alarm screams — the Gozanti changed course. It's not running a sweep anymore. It's heading directly for the platform. Someone on that cruiser picked up the docking signature. You have maybe twelve minutes before they're in tractor range. But Threel grabs your arm: there's another platform, a day-camp, eight hours rimward. More families. He has the coordinates. If the Empire finds this platform, they'll sweep the whole sector. Those families will never see it coming.
rounds.narrativeContext: Your ship is overloaded. The hyperdrive will strain under the extra mass. You can make one jump — maybe two if you push it. Threel's coordinates lead deeper into Imperial territory, not away from it.

choices.label: Take the coordinates and jump rimward — if there are more families out there, I have to warn them before the Empire sweeps
choices.narration: You plug in Threel's coordinates and feel the hyperdrive groan under the weight of fifteen extra souls. The stars stretch. You're heading deeper into the dark, not away from it. But somewhere ahead, people are sleeping who don't know what's coming. You'll get there first. You have to.

choices.label: Transmit a coded warning to the rimward camp on the old frequency, then jump to safety — they'll have to save themselves
choices.narration: You key the burst transmission. Same old codes. Run. Hide. Trust no one who doesn't know the frequencies. Then you plot a course away — somewhere safe, somewhere anonymous. You've done what you can. The signal will reach them or it won't.

choices.label: Jump to the nearest safe port — these people on my ship are my responsibility now, and I won't risk them for strangers I've never met
choices.narration: You slam the hyperdrive lever and the platform shrinks behind you. Threel stares at the navcomputer, watching his coordinates go unvisited. The children in your cargo hold are safe. That's enough. That has to be enough. You tell yourself you'll come back when the heat dies down. You probably won't.

resolutions.light: You emerge from the shadows carrying more weight than any ghost should bear. Your ship groans. Your cover is blown. Every Imperial sensor net in the sector has your engine signature now. But behind you, families breathe who would have suffocated in the dark. The open hand costs the ghost everything — anonymity, safety, the cold comfort of being no one. You chose to be someone. The Shipboard Born remembered what the freighters taught him: you don't fly past people who are drowning.
resolutions.dark: You emerge from the run with your ship intact, your identity clean, and your cargo hold carrying exactly the people you chose to save. The reactor team, the rimward camp, the ones too slow or too far away — they fade into the static behind you. Ghost Protocol. It kept you alive through the Purge, and it kept you alive tonight. The math works. It always works. But somewhere in the quiet of hyperspace, you find yourself listening for a signal that stopped transmitting hours ago. Three short, two long, one short. You'll hear it in your sleep for a while.
resolutions.neutral: You emerge from the run carrying compromises in both hands. Some were saved. Some weren't. You sent warnings where you could and sealed doors when you had to. The spacer families will whisper about you in the dark — some with gratitude, some with something harder to name. You did what the situation allowed. Not everything. Not nothing. The Hunted learned long ago that perfection is a luxury the galaxy doesn't sell. You'll keep flying. You'll keep listening. And the next time that frequency crackles, you'll make the same impossible calculus all over again.
